title: An Unlikely Titan of Industry & Commerce: Henry Ford - YouTube
text:
[Music]
good afternoon
everyone welcome to the Flagler Museum
on behalf of the trustees and the staff
my name is uh John blades I'm the
director of the flag Museum it's my
pleasure to welcome you all here today
for the third in our series of lectures
the White Hall lecture Series this
year's theme is the un likely Titans of
industry and commerce and today's
lecture will be on Henry Ford let me
just take a couple of minutes to remind
you to turn off your cell phones to make
sure your cell phones are turned off
these lectures are recorded and archived
and also live stream so if by chance
you're not able to join us in person you
can always join us on a Sunday afternoon
at 3 o'cl via the internet anywhere you
are in the
world um let's see what I'll do I need
to remind you of oh yeah our sponsor our
sponsor for this Le this year's lecture
series is related a developer here in
the area that owns most of the Class A
office space in West Palm Beach and
they've just launched a program called
related together which is a program that
hopes to achieve economic development in
West Palm Beach for some of the
underserved
communities um let's see today's lecture
is
by Stephen Watts Dr Watts has been at
the University of Missouri for a number
of years I guess about three decades and
he just retired if I'm remembering
correctly a little more than three
decades he just raised his thumb uh
where he taught a variety of History
classes and he's written a number of
books on people like Walt Disney Dale
Carnegie Hugh Hefner I think you're just
finished one on on um Will Rogers which
that ought to be interesting so but here
he's here today to talk about Henry Ford
and there'll be books at the back of the
room uh if you care to purchase his book
on Ford and I'm sure he'd be happy to
sign it so without further Ado please
join me in welcoming Dr Stephen watts to
the White Hall lecture
[Applause]
series all right uh well thank you John
for that very kind uh
introduction I am delighted to be here
at Flagler to speak today for a couple
of reasons really uh one it's a great
honor to be included in this very
distinguished lecture Series so thanks
for inviting me secondly I have to
confess I'm delighted to be here because
of the god alul winter we've had back in
the midwest this year um I was telling
them at lunch that about I guess it's
been about three weeks ago now we got
hit by an Arctic Blast for about 10 days
the temperatures were between 0o and 10
during the day and zero and 10 below uh
during the night uh we live on a farm we
have horses and dogs they were frozen we
have chickens they were frozen we live
there we were frozen and I still haven't
quite thought out from all of that so
it's really a delight to be down here in
the warm weather uh even if it's a
little bit rainy today that's that's
completely
okay well this afternoon I want to
discuss uh for a little while uh the
career the significance and the legacy
of Henry Ford uh someone who is I think
arguably one of the two or three most
influential figures in the shaping of
modern American Life uh as we still lead
it today and I want to begin with a
story in 1920 8 uh on the 20th
anniversary of the launch of the Model T
automobile Henry Ford created a public
uproar uh as he liked to do on occasion
uh he was being interviewed by the
Associated Press about his life and
career and they asked about the secret
to success uh as he saw it and Ford said
that the old traditional formula of hard
work and saving money was pretty good
but it was really only half right uh he
said the part about working hard was
still good but the part about saving
money he said was just nonsense uh it
was
fruitless uh successful people did not
save money for it said but and these are
his his words they spent it as fast as
they could for things to improve
themselves well notice of this quickly
went out through the country and there
was a great controversy that erupted
about what the newspapers called Henry
Ford's Gospel of
spending and supporters of his position
um sort of acclaimed his argument uh
that these old Victorian ideals of
thrift self-denial it was time to throw
those in the waist can uh but opponents
of Ford denounced what he had said as
being a kind of dangerous assault on the
virtu of thrift and Prudence which were
still quite necessary they believed well
Ford's Associates and those who followed
his
career however were not really surprised
I think at what he uh what he had argued
see if I can get this up
here a
yeah there we
go anyway those who know Ford quite well
uh were're not surprised Ed at his
expression of this sentiment they knew
that Ford deeply believed in the virtues
of hard work but they also knew that he
believed deeply in what he saw as the
virtues of purchasing and the necessity
of leisure and the necessity of
Manufacturers responding to the wants of
consumers and this enjoyment of material
Goods Ford had said actually throughout
his career led to contentment it led to
new experiences and it led to a richer
life and moreover Ford believed that at
the center of this new ethic in American
life was the automobile uh particularly
the Model T uh which is pictured here
coming out of the Highland Park uh
Factory in Dearborn his first big
Factory by the thousands in the early
20th century Ford believed that the
automobile was at the key at a key
juncture anyway of this new kind of
society and his words were these and
again I quote he said by enabling people
to get about quickly and
easily it gives them a chance to find
out what's going on out in the
world and this leads to a larger life
that requires more food more and better
Goods more books more music more of
everything as a close associate of fors
once noted he said his boss understood
that his life's work was quote a mission
to demonstrate the superiority of an
economy of abundance over one of
scarcity and to begin the elevation of a
standard of living to a height never
before dreamed of well here's a Henry
Ford that we are little acquainted with
I would suggest the Henry Ford that most
of us know the one that we meet in our
history
textbooks um goes something like this
begins with Henry
Ford the man who in the mid
1890s uh built the quadracycle here
pictured uh motoring around on the
streets of New York with its tiny little
engine and its bicycle tires that you
might
note the Henry Ford we know is also the
man who pioneered the assembly line at
his Factory up in the Detroit area where
um tremendous efficiencies of production
were achieved by bringing the work to
the workers instead of the workers to
the work uh in Ford's classic
formulation still later the Ford we meet
in the textbooks is the one who built
the mammoth River Rouge Automobile Plant
out on the Detroit River where raw iron
or came in one end of this enormous
complex and completed automobiles came
out of the other the Ford we know the
Ford were familiar with was a
manufacturer a maker of goods a kind of
Hard Scrabble
businessman whose genius for efficiency
created uh un unprecedented production
and American industrial life Henry Ford
The Familiar one was a producer who put
America on Wheels and created the
automobile centered Society of Highway
systems of suburban life life uh of
suburbs of service Industries
surrounding the automobile all the
things we know that really are the kind
of skeleton of how we still live life
today in American
society now all these traditional images
uh I would admit ring true I think at
least in part but the familiar Ford the
one I've just described I would insist
is really just a shadow of the man that
I noted at the very out set of my
remarks the cultural innovator who
talked about spending and uh consumer
abundance and
self-improvement this Ford shaped new
values a new definition of America
really that like the automobile I think
still holds sway in our society up to
the present time he is I think the
essential Henry Ford that I want to
discuss with you for a while this
afternoon now Ford as some of you may
know had a fascinating and a
multifaceted life and this is something
that still astounds me Henry Ford was
born during the Battle of Gettysburg uh
in the American Civil War but he lived
to see the dropping of the atomic bomb
and the beginning of the Cold War in the
late 1940s kind of an astonishing span
of time he grew up on a farm outside of
Detroit Michigan and this is the kind of
modest Farmhouse that he had
reconstructed uh later on his
parents pictured here William Ford a
fairly prosperous a progressive farmer
uh in the Detroit area his mother Mary
lot Ford uh grew up uh really a kind of
standard World upbringing I think for a
kid in this time period and here we have
the first known picture that we have of
Henry Ford at about age two and a half a
great hat I always love the hat um but
Henry Ford again it's kind of typical
rural upbringing in this period except
for one thing it became a kind of
standing joke in the Ford family that
Henry Ford hated
farmwork especially horses he hated
horses throughout his life he hated the
grind of farm Labor uh he wouldn't do it
his parents had to make him he sort of
groused about it constantly he was much
more interested in mechanical things in
tinkering and he spent most of his time
U repairing watches for example at a
work workbench that he set up in his
home and he spent a lot of other time
sort of looking at and peering into the
new steampowered Farm machines that were
beginning to appear for the first time
in the late 19th century in the rural
areas of the country in
1879 at age 16 Ford did something that
everybody expected he left the farm and
he headed into Detroit which was about
15 miles away where he took a series of
jobs at machine shops and uh sort of uh
crude manufactures in that period And as
shown in this Photograph as a young man
he would be seen uh cycling around the
streets of Detroit to his various jobs
on his
bicycle in
1891 and he was now married to a young
woman Clara Bryant
Ford he found really his first good job
when he was hired as an engineer and a
machinist at the Edison Illuminating
Company in the city a company that was
responsible for bringing electricity to
a good part of the urban
population and uh spent his days there
he is the gentleman by the way to the
left with the mustache with a couple of
his co- engineers at at the illuminating
company but more importantly in the long
run Henry Ford in the evenings had begun
to spend hours and hours working on
something that he had read about in
machinist magazines that he subscribed
to and that is this notion of develop
veloping an internal combustion engine
that would power something that people
had begun to refer to as a Horseless
Carriage and Ford began to develop one
of his own he began to Tinker with it in
a garage behind the house that he rented
with his wife and on Christmas Eve in
1893 he successfully fired up uh his one
cylinder engine uh gasoline engine for
the very first time he got a to work a
little over two years later after a lot
more tinkering he successfully launched
the quadri cycle which I had shown in an
earlier slide here uh from that garage
behind his house and the legendary story
which is true by the way is that uh in
1896 he and a friend of his put the
finishing touches on the quadricycle
they went to wheel it out into the
street and to their horror they saw that
they had forgot to think about how to
get it out the door cuz they've been
building it inside is too big so Henry
Ford literally grabbed a sledgehammer
and had to knock out the bricks around
uh the door of the garage to get the
thing out and get it going in the
company or rather in the street well
over the next several years Henry Ford
tried on two separate occasions to
manufacture an automobile to get a
company up and going both of those
attempts were failures they just didn't
get off the ground at all in 1903
however he secured some investors who
poured enough money into his effort that
he was able to found the Ford Motor
Company in
1903 uh for the first uh three or four
years of the company uh they developed a
couple of prototypes of the automobile
that sold modestly well but in 1908 the
Ford Motor Company hit the jackpot
because in 1908 uh really from Ford's
idea they put out on the market the
Model T
automobile which was a tremendous
success as I'm sure many of you know in
American life this is one of the very
first uh advertisements in 1908 for the
early version of the Model T and this
car basically took the country by storm
it immediately is an inexpensive vehicle
sturdy longlasting began to sell tens of
thousands out there to the uh public and
over the next several years uh well up
into the early 19 mid 1920s sold
millions of numbers to to American
consumers and with the Model T I think
it's fair to say the country was never
quite the same well as Ford became rich
as an automobile manufacturer with the
Model T and the Ford Motor Company he
began to enter the public arena in a
number of different ways and his career
became very
multifaceted in quite a number of areas
for example Ford in the 19s became
probably the most well-known pacifist in
the United States uh during World War I
he actively opposed the War uh when it
uh got up and going and he spent
thousands and thousands of his own
dollars to rent an ocean liner uh which
he called the peace ship and here was a
photo that was taken of him standing at
the rail and the peace ship he loaded up
with anti-war activists and they sailed
off to Europe to try to stop World War I
well no great surprise this didn't work
out too well uh journalists and critics
uh C sort of immediately uh termed this
Enterprise The Good Ship nuty as one of
them called it and it failed miserably
and Ford sort of slunk off the ship in
Europe and came home in 1918 in a kind
of another
departure Ford ran for the
Senate let's try this again
here there we go you 1918 Ford ran for
the Senate was convinced to do so as a
Democrat uh in in that year and although
he barely moved a finger to campaign for
that office he lost by just a whisper to
his Republican
opponent um over the next several years
there was a kind of ground swell of
support for Henry Ford to run for
president he was very very popular but
what sort of killed that was his wife
um Clara Ford who I think knew her
husband better than he probably knew
himself uh sensed that this would have
been an utter disaster and she did an
interview in which she said if Henry
actually ran for president she was going
to move to
England and uh no great surprise Henry
withdrew from consideration a few days
later um Ford also uh in his kind of
multifaceted career in this period
emerged as a reformer kind of a cultural
reformer in a number of different areas
uh he became a kind of outspoken Health
reformer uh he was an opponent of
alcohol consumption he was an opponent
of smoking and tobacco he wrote and had
published a kind of long pamplet which
he entitled the case against the little
white slaver uh which was his name for
cigarettes he was also I guess we would
say a kind of physical fitness nut uh he
rode his bicycle and he ran foot races
with his friends well up into his
70s and uh he promoted vegetarian diets
throughout his life as well uh some of
them quite extreme well in the 1930s
Ford re-engaged with politics with the
Great Depression and the New Deal uh
Ford became a strident critic of
Franklin Roosevelt in the New Deal in
the
1930s uh he beli that the New Deal uh
Roosevelt's big political program for
Recovery was something that was
poisoning individual initiative it was
something in his Ford's view was
transforming too many people into Wards
of the state and uh in this photo here
Roosevelt visited did one of
um Ford's factories in the 1930s and
Ford uh didn't want to do it he was more
or less forced to ride in the back seat
of this automobile with FDR and a tour
of the plant and FDR was his usual
effusive personable self and as you can
get a little hint of there Ford was kind
of sitting in the back of the a
automobile uh looking quite
uncomfortable and a little grouchy about
the whole thing uh he didn't really like
uh like FDR at all at the very same time
in the 30s in concert with this Ford
became a bitter opponent
of of
unionization that was beginning to sweep
through the automobile industry uh
including the Ford Motor Company Ford
bitterly opposed the United Auto Workers
who were trying to organize auto labor
at Ford Motor Company and this led to a
series of very I would have to say sort
of
disastrous uh encounters at his
automobile plants in Detroit where
basically Ford hired people to sort of
beat up uh Union
organizers uh at his plant this is a
photo that was taken at the famous uh
what became known as the battle of the
overpass where a number of these sort of
Ford Company thugs I would have to call
them attacked the organizers for the UAW
and sort of beat them to a pulp uh
during this
encounter uh eventually I should note
Ford relented about all of this because
of economic pressures and eventually
signed an agreement with the UAW but he
wasn't very happy about it well the
1930s also saw however some happier
projects uh in in in Ford's life first
of all he and his staff science ssts up
in
Dearborn um turned to the
soybean uh which has become a kind of
staple of American agriculture but Ford
was the guy who really got soybeans up
and running as a kind of a profitable
cash crop uh for farmers in American
life he and his Engineers also developed
as we see in this photo
here
there we
go um the Ford Ferguson tractor uh which
Henry Ford envisioned is sort of the
model te of farm implements for for
small and mediumsized
farmers and uh Ford believed these kinds
of Innovations which he pioneered would
be very helpful to the agricultural part
of the United States uh who were
suffering terribly many of them during
the Great Depression Ford may also be
interested to know was a kind of Pioneer
in developing vocational schools uh in
Detroit and in Michigan uh he poured
millions of his own dollars into uh
these new kinds of schools which he
established which combined regular
curricular learning on the one hand with
vocational training on the other and I
sort of for good measure threw in a lot
of moral instruction uh for the children
as well and these schools became very
popular uh they were sort of part John
Dewey and part mcguffy's reader I guess
in this period now Ford's private life
uh throughout his career I think was
almost as complex and and interesting as
his public life he enjoyed a long stable
marriage with Clara Bryant Ford and here
they're depicted as one of their
favorite activities dancing uh Ford was
uh a big host of dances at the Ford
complex up there and they spend many
hours at this favorite
Endeavor um Clare Bryant was uh sort of
a a pillar of support I think for Henry
Ford throughout his career uh
particularly during the last decade of
his life when as an old man he suffered
a series of
Strokes that uh really made him a kind
of uncomprehending figure in his own
company uh these Strokes were of course
carefully hidden from the public uh in
order to protect the company and its
values but at the same time uh while
Ford had a long 50-year marriage to
Clara Ford the evidence is pretty clear
that he had a decades long Affair uh
with a much younger woman which resulted
in the birth of an illegitimate son uh
in the
1920s and it also produced a curious
situation uh where neither myself nor
anyone else who has studied Ford was
able to figure out on Henry P Ford's
deathbed in
1947 the first person that his wife
Clara summoned to his deathbed was his
mistress so they were both there so go
figure the great tragedy I think though
of Henry Ford's private life was his
relationship with his son edel uh
pictured here uh this photo was taken in
the late 1920s when they released the
new model A Car which was the successor
to the to the Model
T Henry throughout his son's life uh he
wanted etel to be a kind of carbon copy
of himself uh resilient shrewd hard
driving focused but the Sun from very
early age was sort of cut from a
different bolt of cloth he was Gentile
uh he was interested in art and music he
was very cooperative and team oriented
in his
sensibility Henry Ford installed edol as
the president of Ford Motor Company
around
1920 and then he pushed his son
relentlessly to be tough in that
position but the the method he chose to
do that was rather sad in hindsight he
undercut edel every decision he made
making it clear that he Henry was the
guy really in charge of everything he
tried to get his son to Rebel uh
thinking that this would be the way that
he would Express a kind of toughen
character but edil worshiped his father
and what he did was to absorb the abuse
and sort of inter internalize the
tension that was created edil kept to
his job in stoic fashion even though he
was often miserable and I think if you
look at his face in this photo uh you
can get the slightest hint of uh the
kind of longsuffering quality that edil
reflected the sad conclusion to all of
this is that I think under the pressure
of this edel developed stomach ulcers
very bad ones and shortly thereafter St
cancer and he died at a very young age
in the early 1940s when he was also in
his 40s Henry Ford his father was
devastated when edil died I think all
the more so because he realized at some
level um that his actions over the years
had sort of contributed to his son's ill
health and his
demise so kind of thumbnail sketch of of
Henry Ford's career well I think when
you look at all of this uh I think what
is most striking about Henry Ford when
you dig into it is the tremendous
popularity that he enjoyed with the
public during the long long scope of his
career and this represented a very
significant change in how businessmen
tended to be viewed in American Life uh
older business tycoons like Cornelius
Vanderbilt uh JP Morgan and the like
were denounced by the public as robber
barons but Ford although he was probably
the first billionaire in American
history enjoyed a special bond of
affection even a Kind of Love Affair I
think with a great American public uh
with ordinary Americans that really sort
of transcended all reason Henry Ford I
think it's fair to say became a kind of
genuine folk hero in American Life
moreover it was interesting uh to
discover at least for me that Ford
generated a kind of promiscuous appeal
to people all over the globe during his
career for example socialists such such
as Vladimir Lenin in the Soviet Union
admired Ford
extravagantly and in the 1920s it was
not a rare thing to see uh big photo rhs
of Lenin and Ford side by side in Soviet
Factories at the same time Adolf Hitler
revered Ford as well and he modeled the
Volkswagen on the Model T uh in in in
German economy in the United States the
business tycoon John D Rockefeller the
big businessman he acclaimed the Ford
manufacturing operation as his work
words the industrial Marvel of the age
but at the very same time woodro Wilson
a progressive Democrat who hated
Rockefeller by the way backed Henry Ford
in his Senate run in
1918 leftist intellectuals such as
Charlie Chaplan Aldis Huxley denounced
fordism as they call it the mechanized
labor the assembly line of
fordism but at the very same time in
1940 rer did a survey of actual workers
in American life and those workers
ranked Ford as the modern leader who was
most helpful to labor above Franklin
Roosevelt above the labor leader Walter
Ruther so what are the reasons for this
this spectacular popular appeal that
Henry Ford had well I think trying to
sort of uh unpack all of this and
discover those reasons tells us a lot
about Ford tells us a lot about his
significance in American history and at
least for our purposes this afternoon I
think we can boil them down to three
areas uh three roles that Henry Ford
played in his career that tell us a lot
about why he is such a tremendously
important figure in this period who
shaped I think not only the
socioeconomic structure of the country
but the cultural deepest cultural values
that we have as a people first of all
what I would stress is the first role is
Henry Ford's role in promoting consumer
culture and consumer values in the early
20th
century he did not develop the this
technology
obviously
oh I don't know guys
see I don't know it's just not going
John there K okay right you got the
magic touch
here so anyway Ford as a as a kind of
advocate for Consumer
values this role that Henry Ford played
I think placed him at The Cutting Edge
of historical change in this period
because his pronouncement about the
automobile as being a kind of key
mechanism in this new culture of leisure
and abundance and
consumerism really caught I think the
popular spirit in the early 19th century
when Americans were ready to be done
with a kind of Victorian tradition of
self-control
self-denial Thrift all of those
old-fashioned values Ford believed that
automobiles like his Model T pictured
here with the famous winged pyramid uh
logo that Ford people develop he
believed that the automobile was key to
developing a richer life for common
people and when he released the Model T
he said this and listen carefully to his
words here he Saidi building a motor car
for the great
multitude it will be so low in price
that no man making a decent salary will
be unable to own one and able to enjoy
with his family the blessings of hours
of pleasure in God's Great Open Spaces
the enjoyment the pleasure of the
multitude that's what Henry Ford was
after and that's the key Insight I think
think in his consumer
Vision this attitude
inspired for instance the $5 day some of
you may have heard of in
1914 when in a kind of extraordinary
revolutionary pronouncement Henry Ford
said that his factories would start
paying his workers $5 a day which does
not seem like much to us but it doubled
the wage of automobile workers by one
stroke of the 10 now he did this it's
important to note not so much as a
reward for productive labor but as a
kind of trigger for consumption in this
period because he as he explained it in
the typical plain language for Henry
Ford he said I want to make sure that
the men who make the modal T can afford
to buy one and that is the key Dynamic
here now I think it's important to to
note that Ford saw consumer abundance as
a way to liberate Americans from a
traditional economy of scarcity in an
earlier period in his words again human
demands are increasing every day and the
needs for their gratification are also
this from the
1920s this is as it should be gradually
wives are released from work little
children are no longer longer exploited
for labor everyone becomes free to go
out and find new products new Merchants
new manufacturers who are supplying
Goods to them now a dramatic example and
this is one of the favorite things of
mine that I ran across in doing this
book dramatic example of Ford's mindset
and all of this came from something I
found in the Ford archives and it's um
an advertising campaign that they
launched in the early 1920s Ford's
advertising team went out new ad
campaign they wanted to launch
nationally and they came up to Henry
Ford's office the story went with the
campaign they put it on his desk and
they drew their cue from the inexpensive
price tag of the Model T and their
original phrase was buy a Ford and save
the difference buy a Ford and save the
difference
well the story went they put that down
at his giest so Henry Ford kind of
looked at it he rubbed his chin he was a
man of few words uh he went over to the
window he looked out the window for a
while and then without saying a word he
came back picked up a pencil and he
scratched out the word save and he
replaced it with the word you see here
spend that onew replacement I think
speaks volumes about uh his thinking
with regard to a consumer culture buy a
Ford said said Henry Ford and spend the
difference not save it spend it and that
one word I think uh really gives us kind
of insight into what's going on here so
I think it's crucial for us to
understand that Ford's productive
Endeavors The Familiar Henry Ford all of
that stuff is very important to be sure
but I think it's more important for us
to understand that he's one of the very
first people to articulate in a
fundamental and convincing way a
consumer vision of not only the American
economy but American life he understood
I think sooner than about anybody that
mass
production depended on mass consumption
and that mass consumption is what you
needed to get going so in the same way I
think uh that his other endeavors move
things forward when what you see here is
that the Model T rather than just being
a new mode of
transportation was a kind of vehicle for
a new vision of the good life as Henry
Ford defined it one of material
abundance Leisure personal contentment
through purchasing something into modern
Consciousness so the Legacy was not just
a change in how Americans lived but
really A redefinition I think of the
American Pursuit of Happiness and what
it meant so that's role number one role
number two that I want to stress and
talk about here for a
while is that Henry Ford emerged in the
early 20th century as one of America's
first mass culture
celebrities uh in American Life After
all if you think about it the early
1900s
was a period that witnessed the
formation of a new kind of mass culture
of entertainment in our country uh
movies amusement parks such as Coney
Island popular music
Halls uh competitive sports teams and
the like as part of this new culture of
consumption and Ford gained a kind of
foothold I think in a lot of things that
he would later do by uh focusing on one
aspect of this new commercial culture of
entertainment we'll give this to the man
with the Magic Touch
here
I don't know
where yeah there we
go anyway Henry Ford got involved with
this new uh sort of commercial cultural
entertainment with automobile racing and
here Ford this is pre Ford Motor Company
uh he was involved with the famous uh
race car driver in this period some of
you may have heard of Barney olfield
they used to call my grandmother that
because she was a kind of pedal to the
metal type of woman
um Barney olfield was the most famous
race car driver of the period and Ford
developed a race car prototype for him
and uh Henry Ford and Oldfield won a
whole bunch of races set a bunch of
Records up in the Detroit area with
racing now the reason this is important
is Ford a got a lot of national
publicity about this but B he also
attracted investors to the Ford Motor
Company because they uh sort of thought
well if he's successful in this this guy
